risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The company randomized the investigational product." Comment: Although the exact algorithm is not described, the paper explicitly states that the investigational product was randomized by the company, and participants were described as randomized upon enrollment. This provides context indicating a systematic random process rather than a predictable rule, so Low risk is appropriate under the permissive criteria.

_evidence.method_quote: Upon enrollment, subjects were randomized and provided with a bottle of the investigational product. ... The company randomized the investigational product.
_evidence.method_summary: Subjects were randomized to receive one of three investigational product doses; the company randomized the investigational product.
context_clues: The company randomized the investigational product

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The company randomized the investigational product. The researchers and study subjects were not provided with the randomization information until all data had been collected." Comment: This explicitly states that the allocation information was withheld from both researchers and participants until after data collection, which is an allocation concealment mechanism that prevents foreknowledge of the upcoming assignment.

_evidence.concealment_quote: The company randomized the investigational product. The researchers and study subjects were not provided with the randomization information until all data had been collected.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The company randomized the investigational product. The researchers and study subjects were not provided with the randomization information until all data had been collected." Comment: This indicates both participants and study personnel were kept unaware of assignment during the trial, consistent with successful blinding. The intervention was also placebo-controlled (no rapamycin vs 0.003% vs 0.015%), and the main outcome here was assessed across both objective measures (e.g. serum rapamycin, CBCs) and some subjective self-reported outcomes; because blinding was maintained, performance bias is unlikely.

_evidence.blinding_quote: "The company randomized the investigational product. The researchers and study subjects were not provided with the randomization information until all data had been collected."
_evidence.blinding_method: placebo-controlled topical investigational product; three doses of rapamycin compounded with Skincerity®: (i) no rapamycin; (ii) 1 mg of rapamycin per 30 cc (0.003%); or (iii) 5 mg of rapamycin per 30 cc (0.015%)

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The researchers and study subjects were not provided with the randomization information until all data had been collected." Comment: The study does not explicitly say the outcome assessors were blinded, but the key subjective efficacy outcome was patient self-report: subjects were asked if they "got better on the treatment", "got worse on the treatment", or if "the treatment made no difference". Because the patient is the assessor for these outcomes, and participants were not provided with the randomization information only until after data collection (i.e. they were not clearly blinded during outcome assessment), knowledge of allocation could influence the reported skin-lesion response. Objective outcomes (serum rapamycin concentrations, complete blood counts) do not offset this worst-outcome subjective outcome.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: The researchers and study subjects were not provided with the randomization information until all data had been collected.

outcomes.label: lesion appearance / "got better on the treatment"

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Two subjects withdrew from the study, secondary to discomfort with product application (a burning sensation with application). Two subjects were withdrawn by the investigators, secondary to poor compliance with study protocols. One subject was withdrawn following prolonged hospitalization unrelated to the study product. Twenty-three subjects completed the entire study protocol." Comment: The study reports 5/28 post-randomization withdrawals overall (about 18%), with stated reasons and at least one reason explicitly unrelated to the study product. Although per-arm numbers are not provided, the reported attrition is not extreme and there is no evidence here of substantial differential or outcome-related loss that would bias the outcome assessment; the main analysis appears to be based on completers.

_evidence.notes: The article reports only overall attrition: 'Two subjects withdrew from the study, secondary to discomfort with product application (a burning sensation with application). Two subjects were withdrawn by the investigators, secondary to poor compliance with study protocols. One subject was withdrawn following prolonged hospitalization unrelated to the study product. Twenty-three subjects completed the entire study protocol.' No per-arm randomised or analysed counts were provided.
